cards.text: An if statement has the form: {{c1::if}} ({{c2::condition}}) {{c3::statement}}

cards.question: What does this code print when x is 5?
cards.explanation: The condition (x == 5) is true, so the statement executes.

cards.text: The {{c1::else}} keyword provides an alternative statement to execute when the if condition is {{c2::false}}.

cards.question: What does this print when x is 3?
cards.explanation: x == 5 is false, so the else block executes.

cards.text: To execute multiple statements in an if, use a {{c1::block}} (compound statement) with {{c2::curly braces}}.

cards.question: Is this code correct?
cards.answer: Yes, both statements execute when x == 5
cards.explanation: The curly braces create a block, so both statements are part of the if.

cards.concept: Why always use blocks with if statements
cards.explanation:
Best practice: Always use curly braces for if/else bodies because:
  • Prevents bugs when adding statements later
  • Makes code structure explicit
  • Easier to read and maintain
  • Avoids indentation mistakes

cards.text: To chain multiple conditions together, use {{c1::else if}} to test another condition when the first is false.

cards.question: What does this print when x is 7?
cards.explanation: x < 5 is false, but x < 10 is true, so 'medium' prints.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
if (x = 5)  // intending to test equality
    std::cout << "five";
cards.explanation: Single = is assignment, not comparison. This assigns 5 to x and always evaluates to true. Should be x == 5.

cards.front: What does this code do: if (x == 5); doSomething();
cards.back: The semicolon creates a null statement. doSomething() always executes regardless of the condition (bug).

cards.text: The condition in an if statement must evaluate to a {{c1::Boolean}} value (true or false).
